content:
# Joshua Chapter 11 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 235-236 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_011_page_001.jpg`

---

1 And it was, when Yavin king of Chatsor heard, and he sent to Yovav king of Madon, and to the king of Shimron, and to the king of Akhshaf,

2 and to the kings who [were] in the north, in the hill-country, and in the Aravah south of Kinnerot, and in the lowland, and in the heights of Dor on the west,

3 the Kena'ani on the east and on the west, and the Emori, and the Chitti, and the Perizzi, and the Yevusi in the hill-country, and the Chivvi beneath Chermon in the land of Mitspah.

4 And they went out, they and all their camps with them, a people great, as the sand that [is] upon the lip of the sea for multitude, and horse and chariot very many.

5 And all these kings met together, and they came and encamped together at the waters of Merom, to fight against Yisra'el.

6 And YHWH said to Yehoshua: "Do not fear before them, for tomorrow about this time I [am] giving all of them slain before Yisra'el. Their horses you shall hamstring, and their chariots you shall burn with fire."

7 And Yehoshua came, and all the people of war with him, against them by the waters of Merom suddenly, and they fell upon them.

8 And YHWH gave them into the hand of Yisra'el, and they struck them and pursued them unto great Tsidon, and unto Misrefot-Mayim, and unto the valley of Mitspeh eastward, and they struck them until he had not left to them a survivor.

9 And Yehoshua did to them as YHWH had said to him: their horses he hamstrung, and their chariots he burned with fire.

10 And Yehoshua turned back at that time and captured Chatsor, and its king he struck with the sword; for Chatsor formerly [was] the head of all these kingdoms.

11 And they struck every soul that [was] in it with the edge of the sword, devoting [them] to destruction; there was not left any breathing [thing], and Chatsor he burned with fire.

12 And all the cities of these kings, and all their kings, Yehoshua captured, and he struck them with the edge of the sword; he devoted them to destruction, as Moshe the servant of YHWH had commanded.

13 Only, all the cities standing upon their mound — Yisra'el did not burn them, except Chatsor alone; [that one] Yehoshua burned.

14 And all the spoil of these cities and the livestock, the sons of Yisra'el plundered for themselves; only, every human they struck with the edge of the sword until they had destroyed them; they did not leave any breathing [thing].

15 As YHWH had commanded Moshe his servant, so Moshe commanded Yehoshua, and so Yehoshua did; he did not turn aside a word from all that YHWH had commanded Moshe.

16 And Yehoshua took all this land: the hill-country, and all the Negev, and all the land of Goshen, and the lowland, and the Aravah, and the hill-country of Yisra'el and its lowland,

17 from the Mount Chalaq that goes up to Se'ir, and unto Ba'al-Gad in the valley of the Levanon beneath the Mount Chermon. And all their kings he captured, and he struck them and he put them to death.

18 Many days Yehoshua made war with all these kings.

19 There was not a city that made peace with the sons of Yisra'el except the Chivvi, the inhabitants of Giv'on; the whole they took in battle.

20 For from YHWH it was to strengthen their heart to meet in battle against Yisra'el, in order to devote them to destruction, so that there would be no grace for them, but in order to destroy them, as YHWH had commanded Moshe.

21 And Yehoshua came at that time and cut off the Anaqim from the hill-country, from Chevron, from Devir, from Anav, and from all the hill-country of Yehudah, and from all the hill-country of Yisra'el; with their cities Yehoshua devoted them to destruction.

22 There was not left [any] of the Anaqim in the land of the sons of Yisra'el; only in Azzah, in Gat, and in Ashdod they remained.

23 And Yehoshua took all the land, according to all that YHWH had spoken to Moshe. And Yehoshua gave it for an inheritance to Yisra'el according to their divisions by their tribes. And the land rested from war.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v1 — יָבִין מֶלֶךְ חָצוֹר (Yavin melekh Chatsor):** The name Yavin (יָבִין) means "he understands/discerns." Chatsor (Hazor) was the dominant city-state of northern Canaan. The Leningrad Codex spells the name consistently as חָצוֹר. The verbal form וַיִּשְׁלַח ("and he sent") introduces the coalition-building that drives the chapter.

2. **v2 — כִּנְּרוֹת (Kinnerot):** The plural form of Kinneret, referring to the region around the Sea of Galilee (later כִּנֶּרֶת). The MT reads נֶגֶב כִּנְּרוֹת ("south of Kinnerot"), locating the Aravah section specifically south of the lake. The term נָפוֹת דּוֹר ("heights of Dor") designates the coastal ridgeline near the Carmel headland.

3. **v3 — תַּחַת חֶרְמוֹן (tachat Chermon):** "Beneath Hermon" — the Chivvites here are distinct from those of Giv'on (v. 19). The land of Mitspah (מִצְפָּה) denotes a watchtower region at the foot of Hermon, likely the Beqa valley.

4. **v4 — כַּחוֹל אֲשֶׁר עַל־שְׂפַת הַיָּם (kachol asher al-sefat hayyam):** "As the sand that is upon the lip of the sea" — the Hebrew שְׂפָה (lip/edge) is rendered literally rather than the conventional "shore." This hyperbolic simile echoes the Abrahamic promise (Gen 22:17).

5. **v6 — עִקֵּר (iqqer):** "Hamstring" — the Piel of עקר, meaning to sever the hind leg tendons of horses, rendering them useless for war. YHWH commands the deliberate crippling of captured military assets, preventing Yisra'el from relying on chariotry (cf. Deut 17:16).

6. **v8 — מִשְׂרְפוֹת מַיִם (Misrefot-Mayim):** Literally "burnings of waters" — possibly a site with hot springs or lime kilns near the coast. The toponym is preserved untranslated as a proper noun. The phrase עַד־בִּלְתִּי הִשְׁאִיר־לָהֶם שָׂרִיד ("until he had not left to them a survivor") uses the Hiphil of שׁאר with the emphatic negative construction.

7. **v10 — לְפָנִים (lefanim):** "Formerly" — indicating Chatsor's prior status as regional hegemon. The phrase רֹאשׁ כָּל־הַמַּמְלָכוֹת הָאֵלֶּה ("the head of all these kingdoms") confirms its political preeminence in the northern coalition.

8. **v11 — הַחֲרֵם (hacherem):** The Hiphil infinitive absolute of חרם, "devoting to destruction" — the cherem ban requiring total annihilation. Rendered as a participial phrase to preserve the infinitive absolute force alongside the finite verb וַיַּכּוּ ("and they struck").

9. **v13 — עַל תִּלָּם (al tillam):** "Upon their mound" — the singular תֵּל (tel/tell) with third-person plural suffix. Cities built on accumulated occupation layers (tells) are distinguished from Chatsor, which alone was burned. This archaeological detail explains why most Canaanite city-mounds show no Late Bronze destruction layer.

10. **v15 — Chain of command:** The verse establishes a three-link chain: YHWH commanded Moshe, Moshe commanded Yehoshua, Yehoshua obeyed. The emphatic repetition of צִוָּה ("commanded") three times and the concluding לֹא הֵסִיר דָּבָר ("he did not turn aside a word") stress unbroken obedience.

11. **v20 — לְחַזֵּק אֶת־לִבָּם (lechazzeq et-libbam):** "To strengthen their heart" — the Piel infinitive of חזק. This theological notice explains the universal Canaanite hostility as divinely orchestrated hardening (cf. Exod 4:21 on Pharaoh). The term חִנָּה (chinnah, "grace/mercy") negated here means that no terms of surrender were available to them.

12. **v21 — עֲנָקִים (Anaqim):** The giant clan descended from Anaq. Yehoshua's campaign eliminates them from the Judean hill-country, but the note in v. 22 preserves their survival in three Philistine cities: Azzah (Gaza), Gat (Gath), and Ashdod — a detail that foreshadows Golyat of Gat (1 Sam 17).

13. **v23 — וְהָאָרֶץ שָׁקְטָה מִמִּלְחָמָה (veha'arets shaqetah milmilchamah):** "And the land rested from war" — the root שׁקט denotes a settled quietness, not merely the absence of fighting but a state of repose. This closing formula marks the end of the conquest narrative proper before the tribal allotments begin (chs. 13–21).

14. **LXX comparison (Vaticanus):** The Septuagint at Joshua 11 follows the MT closely but shows several notable divergences. In v. 1 the LXX adds τὸν βασιλέα Μαρρων ("the king of Marron") as an additional coalition member. In v. 8 Vaticanus reads Μασερων (Maseron) for MT's Misrefot-Mayim. In v. 21 the LXX sequence of cities differs slightly and includes Καδης (Kadesh) where the MT does not. The summary formula of v. 23 is essentially identical in both traditions, confirming the chapter's structural integrity across textual witnesses.
